text:
Create a web-app that uses Google Photos API to help the user manage their photos.

0. The user should be able to login using Google login.

1. The app should allow the user to create and rename photo albums.

2. The UI should list albums on the left side, and one photo on the right side.

3. The photo on the right side is sourced from the list of all the photos in Google Photos, in chronological order.

3.1. By default, the chronological order should be most-recent photos first.

3.2. The user can navigate to the previous or next photo using the left or right arrows, respectively.

4. Each album on the left has a unique letter of alphabet associated with it.

4.1. The letter associated with an album is derived from the name of the album.

    For example, if the name of the album is 'Family', then associate the letter 'F'; 'Maple' => 'M'; Office => 'F'; 'London Trip' => 'L'; 'Love' => 'V'; 'Baby' => 'B'; 'Baby Shower' => 'S'; etc.


5. When the user presses a key associated with an album, the photo is added to that album.

5.1. A photo can be associated with more than one album.

5.2. All the albums that the photo in right pane is part of, show a green check-mark.

Use TypeScript for this project. Writes tests to make sure that the above listed features are all implemented.

Create a web-app named EZGPhotos that uses Google Photos API to help the user manage their
photos.

0. The user should be able to login using Google login.

1. The app should allow the user to create and rename photo albums in Google
   Photos.

2. The UI should list albums on the left side, and one photo (target photo) on
   the right side.

3. The target-photo on the right side is sourced from the list of all the photos in
   Google Photos, in chronological order.

3.1. By default, the chronological order should be most-recent photos first.

3.2. The user can navigate to the previous or next photo using the left or right
     arrows, respectively.

4. Each album in the left pane has a _unique_ letter of alphabet associated with it.

4.1. The letter associated with an album is derived from the name of the album.

  For example, if the name of the album is 'Family', then associate the letter
  'F'; 'Maple' => 'M'; Office => 'F'; 'London Trip' => 'L'; 'Love' => 'V'; 'Baby'
  => 'B'; 'Baby Shower' => 'S'; etc.

4.2. The letter associated with an album is listed to the left of the name of
     the album.

5. When the user presses a key associated with an album, the target-photo is
   added to that album.

5.1. A photo can be associated with more than one album.

5.2. All the albums that the target-photo is part of, show a green check-mark.


6. If the target-photo is already part of one of the photo albums, pressing the
   key associated with that album removes the target-photo from that album, and
   removes the green-check from that album.

Use TypeScript for this project. Writes tests to make sure that the above listed
features are all implemented.

From your thinking-out-loud above, it looks like you used 'Google Photos Library API', which does _not_ allow a 3rd-party app to manage a user's existing photos; only those photos that the app itself has created/uploaded. If my understanding is correct, please see if 'Google Photos Picker API' is useful for our goal; from my reading of the 'Google Photos Picker API' reference, it doesn't look like the right choice, but I might be wrong. Please investigate how we can help the user with our intended features; perhaps implement an app/website that sits as a proxy between the user and the Google Photos website, and injects code into the HTTP/HTML responses that in turn let the user perform the desired actions.
Please develop a browser extension that implements our requirements (which are also listed in the file Requriements.md).
How do I load this extension in Firefox to test it locally?

The extension pops up a dialog 'Sign in to Google Photos', even though I'm already logged in. And clicking on the 'Login with Google' button in that pop-up opens a new tab with address https://photos.google.com/login, which redirects me back to my Google Photos account (because I'm already logged-in). I've verified this behaviour in Chrome as well as Firefox.
Add a test to prevent this buggy behaviour in future.

The extension's pop-up should start out in closed state, shown as a small square in the top-right corner. The square should be draggable to any part of the screen within the browser, and stay fixed there irrespective of the scroll position of the window. It should open up to full-screen mode only when it's clicked. In the top-right corner of the full-screen mode, it should show the string 'close - [X]', clicking on which leads to turn the pop-up back into the floating square button.

When the extension is first laoded on the new page, it should navigate to the https://photos.google.com/albums URL to gather the list of albums, and then navigate back to the page that was initially opened. If the initial page opened was https://photos.google.com/albums then no need to navigate; neither the first time, nor the second time.
The extension doesn't seem to retain the full list of gathered album names when it navigates back to the originally opened URL.
Remove the ability to rename albums from the extension.

Just a note for the record. The 'Target Photo' region _always_ showed the blurry Google-account profile photo, irrespective of what photo or URL was active.

Please remove the right pane that shows 'Target Photo'. The target-photo is the one that's currently shown by Google Photos; that is, whenever the URL matches the https://photos.google.com/photo/ prefix, assume that the target photo is in focus.
For an album named "2019 Father's Day" I see the name "2019 Father's Day4 itemsMore options" in the list. This seems to be the behaviour for all the albums.
That doesn't seems to have fixed it.

This is what the HTML of that album looks like.
2019 Father's Day
4 items
Other suffixes that still show up in album names are: 191 items · Shared No items
The element class names are not guaranteed to be stable, so I would not like the code to depend on those names.
Why did you add `vite.config.js` in `.gitignore`.
Please do.
Are there any files in the repository that are not listed in .gitignore file, and can be removed from the project without any ill effects?
Thanks! I have removed index.html and tsconfig.app.json. I need to keep the other 2 files. Please run the tests and build commands to ensure nothing breaks because of deleted files' absence.
